title: Ensure Kubernetes secrets data is encrypted with keep  your own key (KYOK)

input_row.title: Ensure Kubernetes secrets data is encrypted with keep  your own key (KYOK)
input_row.description: IBM Cloud® Hyper Protect Crypto Services (Hyper Protect Crypto Services for short) is a dedicated key management service and hardware security module (HSM) based on IBM Cloud. This service allows you to take the ownership of the cloud HSM to fully manage your encryption keys and to perform cryptographic operations. Use Hyper Protect Crypt Services, a dedicated key management service (KMS) and hardware security module (HSM), to encrypt data in Kubernetes secrets and prevent unauthorized users from accessing sensitive app information, for example, credentials and keys.
input_row.rationale: You can encrypt data with customer managed keys in Kubernetes secrets to prevent unauthorized users from accessing sensitive app information. Using your own encryption root key adds a layer of security to your etcd data and Kubernetes secrets and gives you more granular control of who can access sensitive cluster information. When it comes to encryption of data at rest in IBM Cloud, we recommend for customers to manage customer root keys (CRK) that are used to protect customer data stored in data and storage services in IBM Cloud. With the support of Keep You Own Key (KYOK), customers can maintain exclusive control of their HSMs and their keys. Also, customers can manage lifecycle of their customer root keys where they can create, rotate, delete those keys. This provides a significant level of control where those CRKs are managed by the customer, and exclusive control of the HSM, which in turn increases both security control as well as meet relevant compliance requirements. These CRKs are used in turn to protect the data encryption keys (DEK) used to encrypt the data. KYOK is supported by Hyper Protect Crypto Services and is the only service in the cloud industry that is built on FIPS 140-2 Level 4-certified hardware
input_row.audit: 1. Install the etcd CLI (etcdctl) version 3 or later. 2. Set your terminal session context to use the appropriate etcdctl API version. export ETCDCTL_API=3 3. Run the ibmcloud ks cluster config command and include the --admin option, which downloads the etcd certificates and keys for your cluster, and the --output zip > .zip option, which saves your cluster configuration files to a compressed folder. ibmcloud ks cluster config -c --admin --output zip > .zip 4. Decompress the compressed folder. 5. Get the server field for your cluster. In the output, copy only the master URL, without https:// and the node port. cat .//kube-config.yaml | grep server 6. Get the etcdPort for your cluster. ibmcloud ks cluster get -c --output json | grep etcdPort 7. Get the name of a secret in your cluster. kubectl get secrets [-n ] 8. Confirm that the Kubernetes secrets for the cluster are encrypted. Replace the secret_name, master_url, and etcd_portfields with the values that you previously retrieved, and replace with the name of the cluster in your compressed folder file path. etcdctl get /registry/secrets// --endpoints https://: --key=".//admin-key.pem" --cert=".//admin.pem" -- cacert=".//ca.pem" 9. Ensure the output is unreadable and scrambled, indicating that it is encrypted.
input_row.remediation: From Console: 1. Log in to your IBM Cloud account. 2. To view the list of services that are available on IBM Cloud, click Catalog. 3. Search for Hyper Protect Crypto Services. 4. Select a service plan, and click Create to provision an instance of Hyper Protect Crypto Services in the account, region, and resource group where you are logged in. 5. To view a list of your resources, go to Menu > Resource List. 6. From your IBM Cloud resource list, select your provisioned instance of Hyper Protect Crypto Services. 7. To create a new key, click Add key and select the Create a key window. Specify the key's name and key type. 8. When you are finished filling out the key's details, click Create key to confirm. 9. From the Clusters console, select the cluster that you want to enable encryption for. 10. From the Overview tab, in the Summary > Key management service section, click Enable. 11. Select the Key management service instance and Root key that you want to use for the encryption. 12. Click Enable. 13. Verify that the KMS enablement process is finished. From the Summary > Master status section, you can check the progress. 14. After the KMS provider is enabled in the cluster, data in etcd and new secrets that are created in the cluster are automatically encrypted by using your root key. 15. To encrypt existing secrets with the root key, rewrite the secrets. This cannot be done from the console. See the From Command Line section. From Command Line: 1. Log in to IBM Cloud through the IBM Cloud CLI. ibmcloud login [--sso] 2. Select the region and resource group where you want to create a Hyper Protect Crypto Services instance. ibmcloud target -r -g 3. Provision a public or private Hyper Protect Crypto Services instance. ibmcloud resource service-instance-create kms tiered-pricing [-p '{"allowed_network": "private-only"}'] 4. Create a customer root key (CRK) in your KMS instance. You can't use the CLI for this action, you must use the GUI or API. See the above From Console section. 5. Get the ID of the KMS instance that you previously created. ibmcloud ks kms instance ls 6. Get the ID of the root key that you previously created. ibmcloud ks kms crk ls --instance-id 7. Enable the KMS provider to encrypt secrets in your cluster. Fill in the options with the information that you previously retrieved. The KMS provider's private service endpoint is used by default to download the encryption keys. To use the public service endpoint instead, include the --public-endpoint option. The enablement process can take some time to complete. ibmcloud ks kms enable -c --instance-id --crk [--public-endpoint] 8. Verify that the KMS enablement process is finished. The process is finished when the Master Status is Ready. 9. After the KMS provider is enabled in the cluster, data in etcd and new secrets that are created in the cluster are automatically encrypted by using your root key. 10. Set the context for your cluster. ibmcloud ks cluster config -c 12. With cluster-admin access, rewrite the secrets to encrypt them. kubectl get secrets --all-namespaces -o json | kubectl replace -f -
input_row.impact: You cannot disable KMS provider encryption. Do not delete root keys in your KMS instance, even if you rotate to use a new key. If you delete a root key that a cluster uses, the cluster becomes unusable, loses all its data, and cannot be recovered. Similarly, if you disable a root key, operations that rely on reading secrets fail. Unlike deleting a root key, however, you can reenable a disabled key to make your cluster usable again. If you lose your key, you will not be able to recover the data. Standard pricing for Hyper Protect Crypto Services applies.
input_row.default_value: Kubernetes secrets are Base64 encoded but not encrypted.

step1_initial_assessment:
**AUDIT APPROACH:** Automated

**APPROACH DECISION:**  
- The control is focused on encryption of Kubernetes secrets with customer-managed keys via the IBM Cloud Hyper Protect Crypto Services. The audit process involves verifying that the KMS provider is enabled for the Kubernetes cluster and that secrets are being encrypted.
- The audit steps listed in the control include utilizing the `ibmcloud ks` CLI commands to check cluster configuration and the encryption status of secrets, which indicates that the necessary IBM Cloud APIs and CLI commands are available for automated verification.

**PROGRAM NAME:** ibm_kubernetes_cluster_kms_encryption_enabled

**AUTOMATION DETAILS:**  
- Perform an API/CLI check to verify that the KMS provider is enabled for Kubernetes secrets encryption in the cluster.
  
1. **IBM Cloud CLI Commands**:
   - Use `ibmcloud ks cluster get -c --output json` to retrieve cluster configuration. Check the configuration for the `kms` section to ensure it is enabled.
   - Use `ibmcloud ks kms instance ls` to list KMS instances and confirm that the appropriate KMS instance is associated with the cluster.
   - Use `kubectl get secrets --all-namespaces` to list the secrets and further verify the encryption status.

2. **Validation Logic**:
   - Ensure that the response of `ibmcloud ks cluster get -c --output json` includes a section indicating KMS is enabled.
   - Confirm that the KMS instance listed in the output is correct and pertains to Hyper Protect Crypto Services.
   - Validate that secrets returned by `kubectl` show their encryption status as unreadable or scrambled, indicating they are encrypted.

3. **Expected Pass Criteria**:
   - KMS provider is enabled for the cluster (indicated by the `kms` configuration parameter).
   - A valid KMS instance is associated with the cluster.
   - The retrieved secrets do not output sensitive information in a readable format (i.e., they are encrypted).

This structured approach allows for an automated compliance audit of Kubernetes secrets encryption using Hyper Protect Crypto Services on IBM Cloud.
step2_review:
**APPROACH VALIDATION:** PARTIALLY_AGREE

- The classification of the audit approach as Automated is generally correct, given that IBM Cloud APIs and CLI commands are available and can be used to verify the encryption status of Kubernetes secrets. However, the approach should also consider the potential need for manual verification steps in certain scenarios, such as confirming the encryption status of secrets directly within the Kubernetes environment, which might not be fully covered by the automated checks alone. Additionally, the use of `kubectl` to verify encryption status is not inherently automated unless scripted, which could introduce manual elements.

**TECHNICAL ACCURACY:** 7

- The automation details are mostly technically correct, and the IBM Cloud CLI commands listed are valid for retrieving cluster configurations and KMS instances. However, the use of `kubectl get secrets --all-namespaces` to verify encryption status is not directly indicative of encryption, as Kubernetes does not natively show encryption status in secret listings. This step might require additional logic or tools to confirm encryption, such as checking annotations or using a custom script to verify the encryption status of secrets.
- The program name `ibm_kubernetes_cluster_kms_encryption_enabled` is somewhat descriptive but could be more specific to indicate that it checks for encryption of secrets specifically.

**IMPROVEMENTS:**

- The assessment could be improved by including additional steps or scripts to verify the encryption status of secrets, as the current method using `kubectl` is insufficient for confirming encryption.
- Consider using IBM Cloud's logging or monitoring services to track encryption status changes or verify configurations over time.
- The program name could be improved to `ibm_kubernetes_secrets_kms_encryption_status` to more clearly indicate the specific focus on Kubernetes secrets encryption status.
- Explore the use of IBM Cloud's Security and Compliance Center for additional automated checks and reporting capabilities.

**CONFIDENCE:** MEDIUM

- While the approach is generally sound, the reliance on `kubectl` for encryption verification introduces uncertainty, as it does not directly confirm encryption status. Additional verification methods or tools would increase confidence in the assessment.

**FINAL RECOMMENDATION:**

- Modify: Enhance the automation details to include more robust methods for verifying the encryption status of Kubernetes secrets, such as custom scripts or additional IBM Cloud services. Consider revising the program name for clarity and specificity.
- Re-classify: Consider a hybrid approach that includes both automated and manual verification steps to ensure comprehensive compliance with the control.
